claim_text: Next-state signals can serve as a live online learning source across heterogeneous agent interaction types.
evidence_summary: The paper defines next-state signals across personal conversations, terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call settings and shows how each can be routed into the same training loop through a unified MDP framing and infrastructure.
support_strength_reason: This is the central framework claim and is supported directly by the formalization, infrastructure description, and per-setting table.
reviewer_note: The claim is about framework design and supported coverage, not necessarily uniform empirical effectiveness in every possible deployment.

claim_text: Directive next-state signals contain information that scalar rewards alone cannot express and can be exploited with OPD.
evidence_summary: The paper explicitly contrasts Binary RL and OPD, showing that OPD uses extracted hints and token-level advantage rather than only scalar evaluative rewards.
support_strength_reason: The claim follows directly from the method definition and table-level comparison of signal types and supervision granularity.
reviewer_note: This is a structural claim about available supervision signal, not itself a broad performance guarantee.

claim_text: Combining Binary RL and OPD yields stronger personal-agent optimization than either method alone.
evidence_summary: Table 3 shows the combined method scoring well above Binary RL and OPD alone in the student setting after both 8 and 16 update steps, and the paper highlights this as the most effective optimization pattern.
support_strength_reason: The table directly compares the three training variants and the combined method is clearly best in the shown setting.
reviewer_note: Supported within the personal-agent setup shown.

claim_text: OpenClaw-RL supports scalable RL across terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call settings.
evidence_summary: Figure 4 plots positive learning curves across terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call settings, and the setup sections specify models, datasets, and rollout details for these domains.
support_strength_reason: The figure and setup sections show the framework functioning across multiple settings, but the evidence mainly demonstrates viability and positive trends rather than a comprehensive cross-system superiority result.
reviewer_note: Good support for multi-setting applicability, weaker for broader claims of general-purpose dominance.

claim_text: Integrating outcome and process rewards improves over outcome-only optimization for long-horizon agentic tasks.
evidence_summary: Table 4 reports higher scores for integrated rewards than for outcome-only training in tool-call and GUI settings, and the surrounding text interprets this as evidence that process rewards are useful for long-horizon tasks.
support_strength_reason: The reported table does show a consistent improvement in the shown settings, though the evidence is still limited to a small set of task types and values.
reviewer_note: Supports the claim directionally and practically within the reported experiments.

claim_text: A fully decoupled asynchronous RL infrastructure can support online agent training without interrupting serving.
evidence_location: p1 Figure 1; p5 Section 3.1; p18 Appendix A
evidence_summary: The infrastructure diagram, system description, and pseudocode all show policy serving, reward judging, and training operating as separate loops with trainer-queue submission after next-state processing.
support_strength_reason: The architecture is clearly specified and internally coherent, but the visible pages provide more design detail than end-to-end systems benchmarking of interruption-free serving under production load.
reviewer_note: Strong as an architectural claim; less directly benchmarked as an ops-level systems claim.
